feed.title: Today's Darling
feed.subtitle: Daily essays by Shigesato Itoi from 1101.com, translated to English.

entry.title: The people you can see in the background of someone giving their all.
entry.content:
The phrase "it's all thanks to you" is a common one. It was even the title of a variety show, and you hear and read it all the time as a stock expression. But there are also plenty of times when someone says it and truly means it. In fact, come to think of it, I always mean it sincerely when I say it. Truly, from the bottom of my heart: it's all thanks to everyone.

No one can live without the help of others. When I was young, I often felt like I'd done everything myself. I won't entirely begrudge my younger self that kind of youthful pride, but at the same time, there's a part of me that wants to sit that younger me down and say, "That's not how it works." There was so much that happened thanks to other people, and I'm sure I caused plenty of trouble for them too. This isn't a lesson in morality. It's simply a matter of common sense.

Here's an easy example. A professional baseball manager, if he stepped up to the plate, couldn't hit the opposing pitcher's ball. He can't hit better than any of his players, and if he tried pitching or fielding, he'd be so hopeless he couldn't even make it into the game. No matter who you are, the things you can do on your own are few. Everything beyond that small sliver exists thanks to everyone else.

I mean, look, from the moment a baby comes crying into the world, it survives on nothing but the help of others. You can flush the toilet because someone built the sewage system. After your life is over, someone at the crematorium will take care of your remains. I could go on forever, but it's everything, absolutely everything!

Tomorrow, Monday, marks the start of a full week of "The Exhibition of Living Pleasantly." Behind every one of our teammates working as hard as they can, there are families and people who support them. While moms and dads are giving it their all, their children are waiting patiently at home, and their husbands and wives are holding things together. It's because of all those people that we're able to pour our sweat into this work. I'm genuinely, deeply grateful.

All right, it's the last day of preparation. Safety first, fun second. Let's go!

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today. The real work begins once you can see just how much you can't do on your own.
entry.summary: Our individual accomplishments are only possible through the constant support and labor of countless others, from the infrastructure that sustains us daily to the people who love and care for us. True success comes from recognizing this interdependence and feeling genuine gratitude for all those who enable our efforts.

entry.title: Flowers are still blooming.
entry.content:
They say the flower of Japan is the cherry blossom. Whenever Japan comes up, it's always cherry blossoms, as if this were a country where nothing else blooms. And sure, cherry blossoms have that big-star quality about them. But listen.

After the cherry blossoms have scattered, down around knee height as you walk, azaleas burst into vivid color. There are so many of them, blooming everywhere, that nobody ever bothers to have an azalea-viewing party. Even kids today, just like kids from long ago, pluck the azalea flowers and sip the nectar from them. "So sweet!" they say, same as children always have.

Before long, in hedges and garden beds all over town, roses start to bloom. Roses are so popular, and flower shops carry them in every season, so it's hard to notice when they actually start blooming somewhere on the vine. But right around now is the perfect time for planted roses to flower. It's not just the magnificent blossoms worthy of a shop display. The modest, unremarkable ones are lovely too.

Right around the time you start thinking about the rainy season, the plum trees bear fruit. Oh, right, you remember. The Japanese word for the rainy season is written with the characters for "plum" and "rain." The plum blossoms themselves came back in the cold of February, didn't they? Then, depending on where you are, apricots start ripening too. I love apricots. This year I'll almost certainly make apricot jam myself again. Of all the jams in the world, apricot is my favorite.

By the end of the rainy season it's already summer, and all manner of weeds are stretching and spreading with wild abandon. They might be a nuisance, but I envy that kind of energy. And yes, morning glories send their tendrils climbing and open their flowers everywhere. Sunflowers stand tall in the city and the countryside alike. Being big is a perfectly fine thing to be known for, sunflowers.

They say the flower of Japan is the cherry blossom, but the flower calendar keeps going long after the blossoms fall. In every season, actors who may not be stars but who know their craft are blooming out there, whether we bother to look or not. Just as there is always music somewhere, flowers are blooming right now.

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. And my sincere gratitude to everyone who tends the plantings along our streets.
entry.summary: While cherry blossoms dominate Japan's image, the essay celebrates the often-overlooked flowers that bloom throughout the year—azaleas, roses, plums, morning glories, and sunflowers—each beautiful and worthy of appreciation even without fanfare. The author reminds us that subtle beauty and quiet excellence exist all around us if we simply pay attention to what's in season.

entry.title: Writing is hard enough as it is.
entry.content:
No, wait, wait, wait. I just realized something incredible. We live our lives using Japanese, but when someone actually tells us to sit down and write something, more often than not we struggle with how hard it is. (Don't we?) If someone said, "Write about anything you want, around a thousand characters," what would you write, and how? You might think a hundred characters would be easier, but that's hard in its own way. Ten thousand characters? That's enormously, enormously difficult. A thousand, a hundred, a hundred thousand, or even just twenty: writing is hard no matter the length. And when you think about it, this has always been taken for granted. That's exactly why people who possessed the skill of putting things into words were called "good writers" or "authors" or "professionals," and could make a living from it.

From elementary school kids to full-grown adults to seasoned veterans of life, writing has always been a difficult thing. On top of that, writing something grammatically correct, logically consistent, free of typos and missing characters, and interesting on top of all that? I'd say eighty percent of the population would proudly declare, "Can't do it." I'd like to humbly include myself in that eighty percent, despite having spent all these years disguised as a professional and making a career of it.

I write on Hobonichi every single day. Today, same as always, I'm sitting here writing away like it's nothing. But I'm sure that anyone who actually specializes in writing would be furious at the sloppiness and haphazardness of what I keep putting out there for all to see. After all these years of writing, this is where I am. Am I an idiot?

So yes, writing has always been supposed to be hard. And yet AI can do it effortlessly. Without any practice at all, whether it's a thousand characters, a hundred thousand, a hundred, or twenty, it just keeps producing text. Just now, I tried asking it to "write something, anything," changing the specified length each time, running the experiment over and over. Long or short, every piece came out coherent, grammatically correct, and reasonably interesting, all in a matter of seconds.

How is it that this was so hard for us all along?

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. "A night where silence brings you closer than conversation." Written by AI. Touched up by Itoi.
entry.summary: Writing, which has always been difficult for humans regardless of length or skill level, has become effortlessly achievable by AI—raising the profound question of why something so challenging for us comes so naturally to machines.

entry.title: An old man buying tickets late at night. Me.
entry.content:
We have the word "impulse buy," and I suppose this is a variety of that. I'm the kind of person who impulsively buys tickets. Even for events I'd never had any connection to, once I get the feeling that I really ought to see something, I want to go. Back before the internet existed, I was showing up at all sorts of performances and games. Murata Chōji's retirement game at Kawasaki Stadium. Misora Hibari's "Phoenix Concert" at the Tokyo Dome. Pink Floyd at the Hakone Aphrodite. The game where Sadaharu Oh hit home run number 756. RC Succession's debut at the Hibiya Open-Air Concert Hall. I'd get a sudden urge and head out on my own. I suppose you'd call it a rubbernecker's instinct. I have plenty more "I was there" stories to brag about, but I'll stop before I turn into an old man rambling about the past.

Still, this feeling of "I need to see this" has only grown healthier now that you can buy tickets online, because it's become so much easier to act on. I bought tickets to see Kiyoshi Maekawa late one night, all by myself, and went. He spotted me in the audience, and we had a reunion for the first time in twenty years.

Yesterday's "Sayuri Ishikawa Pre-55th Anniversary Concert" was another one of those tickets I clicked on in the middle of the night. Sayuri Ishikawa is apparently close friends with Akiko Yano, and it seems the two of them have gone to baseball games together in the States. On top of that, I once happened to be seated next to her at a Michiko Shimizu show, so we exchanged greetings. But that was about it.

At the end of the day, she's the voice behind this karaoke standard and that karaoke standard. She's someone who has given the world one "classic" of Japanese popular song after another. And yet, it occurred to me that never once having experienced Sayuri Ishikawa live was a real waste. So, striking while the iron was hot, I made the reservation, and headed to NHK Hall with Y-shita, who I'm not even all that close to.

Long story very short: the singing, incredible. The voice, I surrender. I was deeply grateful to the version of myself who had quietly bought those tickets in the middle of the night.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, too. A live performance is something you can never get back once you've missed it. Impulse is the best.
entry.summary: The author celebrates their lifelong habit of impulsively buying tickets to live performances, discovering through a spontaneous decision to see Sayuri Ishikawa that these spur-of-the-moment choices often lead to unforgettable experiences worth treasuring. They argue that acting on the urge to witness live events, especially now that it's easier than ever online, is always worthwhile because these moments can never be recreated once missed.

entry.title:
Source of flavor.
Wait, let me reconsider. "ソース味" refers to the flavor of Japanese Worcester-style sauce (ソース).
Sauce flavor.
entry.content:
When it came to deciding what to feed guests visiting from overseas, the standard answer used to be "sushi and tempura." The guests themselves had heard of these things, so they'd say, in halting Japanese, "I'd love to try that!" Nobody ever opened with "How about some natto?" But gradually, visitors started arriving better informed, and now things like ramen, onigiri, yakitori, and egg sandwiches have become quite popular too. Apparently, the quality of Japanese convenience store food was "discovered" during the Tokyo Olympics, held in the middle of the pandemic. And at some point, the rather formal phrase "guests from overseas" got replaced by the bouncier term "inbound," didn't it.

Every time news reaches us that some big Hollywood star was spotted eating ramen, it's funny how we find ourselves thinking, "Well, of course," feeling oddly proud even though we had nothing to do with making it. Lately I hear that tonkatsu restaurants are drawing plenty of inbound customers too. "Tonkatsu, naturally," I nod, as though I saw it coming.

Once things reach this point, you can't help wanting to guess what'll catch on next. I have a feeling it won't be natto. Tofu, made from the same soybeans, is already well known, and "edamame" has apparently entered the global vocabulary. But natto... that's going to be a tough sell.

Since predictions are futile, let me just go with personal preference. What I'd really like to champion is the vast universe of sauce flavor. Sauce already plays a supporting role alongside tonkatsu and the rest of the fried-food team, but I don't think it's getting nearly enough time in the spotlight. Yakisoba with sauce, not the Chinese-style kind, is wonderful, and the whole appeal of "konamon," those flour-based street foods, comes down to that sauce flavor, if you ask me.

So the next time you're eating out with a friend from abroad, casually steer them toward something with sauce. Don't worry about what good might come of it. Just do it.

Thanks for visiting Hobonichi again today. You know, the range of flavors and aromas in sauce is even more diverse than what you'll find in wine.
entry.summary: As Japanese cuisine gains global popularity beyond traditional dishes like sushi, the author advocates for greater international appreciation of Japan's overlooked culinary gem: sauce-based dishes, which deserve far more recognition than they currently receive.

entry.title: The form of manzai, and fiction.
entry.content:
The format of manzai comedy holds a real advantage in our current age, and that's because you have a partner right there beside you to play the straight man. This means the one playing the fool can say outrageous things, stupid things, irresponsible things, even dangerous things. Because their teammate is right there to say, "No way!" and pull them back to common sense. Even if one half of the duo says something wrong, the team as a whole can claim it still falls within the rules of society. In a time when criticism and opposing views fly in from every conceivable angle, this may be the most survivable form of expression there is.

If a solo performer stood at the microphone and said the same things, those words would be taken as "his opinion," plain and simple, and sometimes held against him as "his mistake." But when you really think about it, comedic talk was never meant to be a statement of personal views. It was supposed to be a craft, an art of making people laugh. And yet, because it overlaps with the speaker's own voice, people are quick to assume, "So that's what this comedian actually thinks." But if you only ever say things that stay safely within the bounds of common sense, you'll never produce a single laugh. In that sense, the manzai format, with its built-in self-criticism, is perfectly suited to an era like ours.

Writing, however, is not a team act. When you say something on the page, it belongs to you alone. You can't have someone jumping in beside you to correct you as you go. Well, you could try, but writing while preemptively apologizing for every possible objection would be so constrained as to be unbearable. And that, I think, is exactly why fiction is so interesting. Characters in a novel are free to transgress. You can give voice to evil, to hypocrisy, to madness. This is a possibility we ought to open up even further.

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. Come to think of it, isn't it true that so much of what we know about being human, we first learned from novels?
entry.summary: Manzai comedy's partnership format allows for bold, transgressive humor that self-corrects through its duo dynamic, whereas solo performance and especially written fiction lack this built-in safeguard—yet fiction's solitary nature is precisely what gives it the freedom to explore the full complexity of human experience without constraint.

entry.title: It might not be worth it, and yet.
entry.content:
The Korean Netflix drama "Everyone Is Fighting Their Worthless Self" is finally reaching its conclusion with Episode 12. As I write this, I've only watched up to Episode 11, and I honestly wonder whether they can wrap everything up in just one more episode (about an hour and five minutes, I think). Maybe it'll end with a feeling of "and the story goes on." Either way, this drama truly pulled me in. I imagine there are plenty of people who haven't seen it yet, so I won't say too much, but I think Park Hye-young's screenplay is simply outstanding. Part of what drew me in may be that the story is about people who make films. These are people who pour fierce passion into filmmaking, living their lives with their individual dreams and emotions all tangled up together. Of course they want a hit, and of course they want commercial success. But without that something more, that passion and seriousness beyond just business, this story wouldn't have worked, and that's exactly what makes it worth watching.

There's only so much you can accomplish when all you think about is profit and loss. When you reduce everything to labor and compensation, there's a whole world of things you simply can't do. So why do these characters throw themselves so completely into something that doesn't even pay off? The show never gives you a direct answer. But without people like them, it's not just film. Every form of expression would stop being refined, would stop surviving into the future.

Come to think of it, Kenji Ozawa recently said about his current live performances, "Playing with young musicians is really stimulating." I thought, yes, of course it is, and at the same time I found myself imagining how even now, in this day and age, there are young people practicing music intensely, choosing performance as their profession. In a way, if you start talking about cost-effectiveness, playing an instrument at that level is a job that just doesn't add up. But it made me happy to know that these "young people" who inspire someone like Ozawa are still out there, still growing. The fact that so many people still enter professions that demand enormous skill and relentless practice, going beyond calculations of profit and loss, beyond labor and compensation, that really is something I'd call "hope."

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. Being able to do what you truly want to do is an extraordinary kind of joy, after all.
entry.summary: The essay celebrates how artists and creative professionals—whether filmmakers or musicians—pursue their passion beyond financial considerations, and argues that this commitment to excellence beyond profit is essential to human culture and what gives life genuine meaning.

entry.title: Isn't there anyone who looks happy?
entry.content:
Is there someone you look at and think, "That person seems so happy"? It could be someone close to you, or even yourself, or some famous person you've never met. Better if they're not fictional, though. And ideally, someone you could actually go and see if you wanted to. What I'm saying is, I'd love to meet one of those people, someone others look at and think, "They seem so happy," and ask them all sorts of things. I haven't worked out any of the details yet. It just came to me.

Yesterday I went to see *The Devil Wears Prada 2*. The movie was good, really entertaining, and clearly a hit. The original, now over twenty years old, is still fun to watch, and pulling off a sequel with the same cast is practically an acrobatic feat. The fact that they managed to put together a film this solid is genuinely impressive. It's not all glamour and lightness, but the dazzling, stylish costumes do their work beautifully.

On the walk home, I was thinking to myself. Why is it that this movie doesn't feel like a happy ending? Every character is trying hard, or being smart, but to me, none of them looked happy. Including the main character. They win, they succeed, and yet there wasn't a single person on screen who struck me as someone I'd describe as "happy."

So then I turned the question on myself. The people I look at and think, "They seem so happy," where are they? Or the people everyone would point to and say, "Now there's someone who's happy," who are they, and where are they living? Do we think that about presidents, or pop stars, or athletes? Among the people I actually know, who would I say that about?

Happiness is subjective in the first place, I suppose, and the more I think about it the more tangled everything gets. But if that's the case, what if I found people who others perceive as happy, and sat down and talked to them about all kinds of things? It's still just an idea I've had on my own, but I'm planning to bring it to everyone and see what they think.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today. I wonder how many treatises on happiness have been written over the course of human history.
entry.summary: The author reflects on the paradox that successful people often don't appear genuinely happy, prompting them to consider interviewing individuals whom others perceive as happy to understand what happiness actually looks like in real life.

entry.title: Remembering that I was once a child too.
entry.content:
Children think strange thoughts. And those strange thoughts, in their own way, end up nourishing something deep inside you later on. I might as well be honest about it now: I was genuinely alert to the possibility that a professional baseball scout, especially one from the Yomiuri Giants, might be watching me. I was particularly vigilant when I stood on the riverbank of the Tone River, throwing stones at the bridge pylons (ostensibly as pitching practice). When something "isn't entirely impossible, when the probability isn't zero," a child's conclusion is: it could happen.

I used to draw on hard-packed ground with wax crayons quite often. If you kept stubbornly filling in a large enough area, the surface of the earth would become slick and smooth, almost as if it had been waterproofed. One rainy day, I was doing just that when I made a "discovery": the rain didn't land on the slippery parts. I had no idea how to articulate this epoch-making law of nature, so I never told anyone. It's been about seventy years now, and I still haven't. Perhaps because I've kept this secret for so long, my thinking is occasionally infiltrated by a kind of inverted logic, along the lines of "a waterproof surface repels rain." Things like "bulletproof vests don't get hit by bullets." Grand, spectacularly wrong ideas. I consider it a personality trait.

Incidentally, I discovered you can buy wax crayons through a certain jungle-themed online retailer, so I ordered some right away. I'm thinking of giving them to my daughter's daughter, but she probably doesn't want them.

I loved books about inventions and was perpetually ready to invent something myself. In third grade, a classmate won a prize at an invention contest. It was obvious his father had made the entry. I was frustrated, but I couldn't say anything about it. Nor did I channel that frustration into working harder. And yet, for all that, I've remembered it ever since. Haven't forgotten it at all. Children, I think now, are the raw material of adults.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. The lies you told as a child, you remember those for a surprisingly long time, too.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on how children's peculiar thoughts, innocent misconceptions, and forgotten moments—from imagining baseball scouts to discovering false "laws of nature"—subtly shape our personalities and persist in our memories well into adulthood. These childhood experiences, whether true or false, become part of who we are and how we think, revealing that children are ultimately the raw material from which adults are formed.

entry.title: Peace without words.
entry.content:
You've probably seen this kind of scene before, at a daycare, a nursery, a park. Tiny children, barely more than babies, playing together, looking happy. Well, they're not always getting along, and sometimes they seem to be ignoring each other entirely. So maybe "playing together" is a stretch. Oh, and dogs at a dog park are the same way. Some are chasing each other around, some are off on their own, some are tumbling together. It's all sorts of things. But either way, in a space that isn't all that big, there's this scene of creatures naturally gathered together. Nobody's in charge. There's no apparent purpose. And there are no "words" to speak of (well, okay, maybe some babbling, some barking). Each of them has their own hunger, their own sleepiness, their own little quarrels. They're all separate individuals, separate dogs. And yet, somehow, they're just naturally there together.

I like watching that kind of thing. And maybe it's not just me. Maybe humans in general like watching it.

I know perfectly well that a world with purpose, with systems and rules, with scheming and strategy and tactics, can be fascinating as a game. That's why I watch dramas, why I watch sports. But there's something about "living creatures, simply being there together" that is its own kind of wonderful, its own kind of quietly heartwarming. I even feel something like longing when I see it.

Especially the part about having no "words." I envy that. I'm someone who has spent his life working with words, and I should know better than anyone how powerful and precious they are. And this very piece I'm writing right now is made of words. But maybe that's exactly why, maybe it's precisely because of that, that I find myself longing for silence.

Dogs and babies can speak with their eyes alone. Sometimes they leap at you, or climb into your arms. These wordless creatures are unbearably dear to me.

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. Words can be weapons, and they can be the red thread of fate that binds us. But so can silence.
entry.summary: The author reflects on the simple beauty of watching creatures—dogs and babies—naturally gather and coexist without language or purpose, and finds in their wordless presence a poignant counterpoint to a life spent working with words.

entry.title: It's not really a "culture festival," you know.
entry.content:
At last, the Life & Enjoyment Exhibition is almost here. There's a certain tension running through the Hobonichi office. Everyone's shifted up a gear in their own way. Of course, the exhibition is work, but it also feels something like a company trip where you actually work. Then again, put it that way and people might think, "That sounds awful." And sure, a company trip where you're forced to work would be the worst. I can't quite put it right. People often compare it to a school festival, but "school festival" doesn't feel like enough. There's unevenness, sure, and some things are done better than others, but what we're doing really is work. Maybe the closest way to describe it is "a place where we go through something together." The crew here at the office, the people exhibiting, the people who come as visitors, the people who show up to help out part-time: everyone goes through it together.

What exactly are we going through? Well, that's the part I can't put into words. It's one of those feelings that resists the kind of "putting it into language" that's so fashionable these days. Each person feels it, and we exchange it, share it. Then, afterward, everyone keeps talking it over, as memories, as reflections, as words, as methods. That turns into the wisdom of "next time, let's try it this way." And the feeling that next time we can do even more becomes something like hope. When it's over, we share the exhaustion too, and rest. You find yourself itching to talk to people about all of it. Then you go back to your regular post, to work that isn't the exhibition. Plans for the next one start quietly taking shape, and the cycle toward the future begins.

Michael Polanyi's idea of "tacit knowledge" was developed by Professor Nonaka Ikujiro into the SECI model, and what we're doing might be close to that. Though it could also be that we're all just kicking around a ball labeled "I can't explain it well, but it's good," and having fun doing it. We're not running things according to theory, so here we are before the big event, bustling around as usual.

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. I'm getting myself ready to go the full distance through that long stretch, in my own way.
entry.summary: The Hobonichi Life & Enjoyment Exhibition creates a unique collective experience that transcends typical work—one where shared effort, unspoken understanding, and post-event reflection generate both immediate fulfillment and hope for future iterations.

entry.title: A place where I can see the one I love.
entry.content:
The place where you can meet the people you love is the place those people love. So if you can figure out what someone loves, you can figure out where to find them. Sometimes people who share the same interests meet as strangers and end up finding someone dear to them.

It sounds like I'm laying out the laws of matchmaking for the internet age, but actually, that opening line, "The place where you can meet the people you love," comes from a jingle I wrote when I was around thirty. It came back to me out of nowhere, and I found myself humming it.

It was 1979. A Seiyu supermarket was opening in front of the train station in Saga, and this was the theme for the grand opening campaign. I went to Saga for location scouting and did some interviews, but everyone there just kept saying, "There's nothing here." From our perspective, we kept thinking, "But it's right in front of the station!" And sure enough, there really were very few people around. "Sometimes a biker gang loops around near the station," they told us. But plenty of people also said, "Once the Seiyu opens, I think folks will start coming, little by little." So there was hope.

If "something" (in this case, a Seiyu) appears in a place where people think there's "nothing," people will gather. Well, that's exactly what the company must have been thinking when they decided to build it. That was more or less my line of reasoning when I wrote the copy. If I remember right, it was: "The place where you can meet the people you love." And that became the lyrics for the opening of the jingle.

I can recall what I was thinking nearly fifty years ago as if it were just the other day. And I realize I'm still thinking about the same kinds of things now. It really doesn't change, does it, the stuff people do.

The place where you can meet the people you love is the place those people love. Everything Hobonichi has been doing all along, the recently opened "Hobo no Eki AKAGI," the "Exhibition of the Joys of Everyday Life" that's about to start. It's all the same thing. It's nothing as fancy as "community building" or "regional revitalization." It's just that people who love the same things want to gather together. That's all.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, too. Incidentally, that Saga Seiyu closed in 2018.
entry.summary: People naturally gather in places centered around what they love, whether it's a supermarket opening in 1979 or a modern community space—the fundamental human desire to connect with others who share your passions remains unchanged across decades.

entry.title: Everyone has more energy than they know what to do with.
entry.content:
It's possible that a great many people are thinking something like this: "If someone would just tell me clearly, 'Do this,' if someone would just give me specific instructions on what I'm supposed to do, I could really show what I'm capable of."

They might also be thinking, "If someone would just present me with a goal, a bright future to work toward, I'd give it everything I've got. I'd work myself to the bone."

And I suspect that's probably true. I think there are an enormous number of people who believe, "The reason I can't perform at my full potential is that nobody's given me something I should be doing, and I haven't found anything I want to do."

In dramas, there's often a leader you can look up to, and the story revolves around the team rallying together to help him succeed. You see it in sports stories, war stories, corporate sagas. The team members in those stories already know what needs to be done. So no matter how hard it gets, no matter how impossible it seems, no matter what sacrifices are required, all they have to do is throw themselves into it with everything they've got.

I imagine some people can't help saying things like, "If I were in that kind of situation, I could get serious too," or "It's because the people at the top don't have a clear vision." I get it. It feels like you've got all this untapped energy just waiting to be unleashed.

But here's the thing. Situations where you know exactly what needs to be done and all that's left is to give it your all, those situations are becoming rarer and rarer in this day and age, whatever it might have been like in the past.

And if what needs to be done is already clear, isn't that exactly the kind of thing AI will end up handling instead of humans?

You have the ladder. You have the strength to climb it. But you don't know where to lean it or where it's supposed to take you. In times like these, actually having something you want to do, something you feel you should do, that's an incredible gift. Maybe creativity is precisely this: making the situation where you can bring your strength to bear.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi again today. First, ask yourself what you want. Even if it's hazy, just try looking at it.
entry.summary: Many people wait for clear instructions or goals from external sources to perform at their best, but in an increasingly complex world where routine tasks are automated, true fulfillment comes from the creative work of defining your own purpose rather than simply executing someone else's vision. The real challenge and gift of modern life is discovering what you genuinely want to do, rather than waiting for someone to tell you what needs to be done.

entry.title: A personal essay: the deliciousness of rice
entry.content:
I won't say every single one, but most sushi rice is delicious, wouldn't you agree? The seasoning of vinegar and a little sugar is part of why, of course, but the grains themselves are delicious too. Each individual grain is smooth and glossy, existing independently. You can really tell that rice is what it is: the seed of a grass, a plant called paddy.

Sushi rice is made by coating freshly cooked rice with seasoned vinegar. And what's crucial in this coating process is the fanning, with a flat fan or the like, to cool the rice down. You're lowering the temperature and driving off the steam. By doing this, the surface moisture is whisked away, and the grains stop getting waterlogged.

I know that was a rather tedious explanation, but I want you to understand this first. The opposite of sushi rice, where cooling and removing moisture makes it delicious, is what happens with "keep warm" mode. While cooked rice sits on the warmer, the steam has nowhere to escape. That steam keeps touching the surface of every grain. Naturally, the grains turn soft and soggy. That's the problem, right there. I want you to feel it. I certainly did.

There are all kinds of theories and methods when it comes to cooking rice. I've tried plenty of them myself, but lately, if I put it as simply as possible, I've arrived at this conclusion: "As long as you don't keep it on warm, it's going to taste pretty good." Transferring it to a wooden rice container is great. Even a non-wooden one works surprisingly well. Shaping it into rice balls is good too. Or after you've stirred the freshly cooked rice, you can just leave the lid off and drape a paper towel over it. You could even scoop it into a bowl and let it cool down that way.

The pleasure of "warm rice" belongs to the moment it's freshly cooked, and that moment only. If you're going to let time pass, skip the warmer and just eat it cold. If you want it hot again, the microwave is a better bet.

I realize you might be wondering what I'm getting so worked up about, but I ended up writing here the kind of thing I'd normally just say to a friend. Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. Think about it: sushi and onigiri are delicious even when they're cold. Of course they are.
entry.summary: The key to delicious rice is removing moisture—whether through the active fanning process of sushi rice or by avoiding the "keep warm" setting that traps steam and makes rice soggy—and understanding that freshly cooked warmth is fleeting, so cold rice is often the better choice.

entry.title: People I admire, who are incredibly resilient.
entry.content:
There are things that sound completely blunt and obvious, yet are fundamentally, incredibly important. For example, something I've written about here a few times: when Takaaki Yoshimoto spoke about why he became interested in Shinran, he said it was largely because he grew up in a household deeply devoted to Jodo Shinshu Buddhism. Or when someone asked him, "Why do you always have the TV on?" and he answered, "Probably because I get lonely." These aren't the kind of impressive-sounding answers people expect, so an interviewer might feel let down. But they get at something truly fundamental. And what I want to say is very much in that same vein.

I saw Kenji Ozawa's incredibly powerful live show, and all I can talk about is the sheer energy of it. I understand what he's going for, what he wants people to feel. But if you think of it in terms of a tree, all of that belongs to the flowers and the fruit. And I'm all for everyone admiring and discussing how magnificent those are. But what I kept sensing was the root force, the fundamental power, root and trunk, that makes those flowers and fruit possible. That's all I wanted to talk about. Bluntly, with no regard for politeness or decorum, what I thought was: "That guy is ridiculously tough!"

He looks like he might be some delicate, sensitive young gentleman, but really he's storing up an almost ferocious amount of energy. It reminds me, I think Seiji Ozawa must have been the same way, just brimming with energy. All the truly extraordinary people in the world have incredible physical stamina. I bet that's true of politicians too, isn't it? And in cases where someone doesn't have that natural stamina, they grind themselves down to nothing, pour out every last ounce of abnormal energy, and essentially burn through their whole life doing it.

Yesterday I went to see Reiwa Roman's big live show, and the energy they put behind every laugh was like a weapon going off. People who can do that kind of thing are the ones who draw others to them. And me? I'm frail, low-energy, and honestly kind of pathetic.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi again today. I'd like to find a way to keep things interesting, even if I stay pathetic.
entry.summary: The essay argues that what truly matters in extraordinary people—whether performers, leaders, or artists—is not their polished output but their underlying physical and mental stamina, the fundamental "root force" that makes everything else possible. The author reflects on his own lack of such energy while appreciating those who possess it abundantly.

entry.title: The lion chases the rabbit with all its might.
entry.content:
I'll begin with a quotation. This may be the first time I've ever done something like this. The source is Aozora Bunko, so you can read the full text there if you like. (I've put the furigana readings in parentheses and added a few line breaks.)

"In the autumn of the year 1902 of the Western calendar, on a forgotten day of a forgotten month, I hoisted a white flag from my bedroom window and sued for peace with the landlady of my boarding house. No sooner had I done so than the old woman set about hauling her one-hundred-and-sixty-pound frame up to the very top of the third floor. I say 'set about hauling' rather than 'hauled' in order to convey just how much effort was involved. The staircase comprised no fewer than forty-two steps. She paused to rest twice along the way. After an expenditure of three minutes and five seconds, the face of this magnificent woman, which ought to have worn an expression of triumph, appeared with agonized abruptness in my doorway. The surroundings shrank to nothing beside her. Addressing myself, who bore the honor of this audience upon my two narrow and burdened shoulders, the old woman, as the first article of our peace terms, issued the following command: Ride a bicycle."

I can't quite work out his system for punctuation, and the writing looks intimidating at first glance, but every line is plainly angling for a laugh. This is the opening of Natsume Soseki's essay "The Bicycle Diary." He blends a grandiose literary style with the patter of a comic storyteller, as if to say: savor my literary craftsmanship until you can't stand it anymore. And that tone carries on, unbroken, for the entire piece. What he's really writing about is simply this: he had to learn to ride a bicycle, and no matter what he tried it was hopeless and he hated every minute of it. Yet somehow the whole thing is rendered like the ornamental transom carving in a newly built house, executed with all the painstaking finesse of a master artisan.

After all, this is Natsume Soseki we're talking about. A great man. His face was on our money. A towering figure of Meiji literature. You might imagine he could dash off something like this with his left hand, scribbling it out like idle doodling, half asleep the whole time.

Yeah, no. I don't think so. He was writing in earnest, moment by moment, with all the seriousness that includes play and pleasure and devotion to craft.

Well, it's not as though I'm reflecting on my own shortcomings here or anything.

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. Soseki wrote this while studying in England at the age of thirty-five. Me? I can ride a bicycle just fine.
entry.summary: The essay celebrates how Natsume Soseki approached the humble subject of learning to ride a bicycle with the same earnest craftsmanship and literary devotion that defined his major works, demonstrating that great writers apply their full artistry to every task, no matter how mundane.

entry.title: Going out to the mailbox to send a postcard late at night.
entry.content:
Before the date could change from May 14th, I walked in my sandals to the nearest mailbox. I wasn't out on some other errand. I was going to mail a postcard. Just minutes earlier, I'd been at the venue for Kenji Ozawa's "Tsuki to Machi no Aidate" concert, and I'd only just gotten home.

"Write anything you want on a postcard and send it to me," he'd said, right at the close of the concert. "As much as possible, today, whatever comes to mind." He said he'd much rather read someone's handwriting on a postcard than neat, easy-to-read fonts on the internet. He went on to explain things like, "If you're in Tokyo, it arrives in a day," and "You don't even need to put a stamp on it, the address is already printed." One after another, these little instructions, all in service of his request: "Please send me a postcard."

The concert was truly wonderful. It was full of colorful schemes and tricks that bordered on mischief, all prepared with a staggering degree of seriousness. The structure of the show, the performances, the singing, all of it was extraordinary in both quality and sheer volume. There's so much I could say about it, and I'm sure someone somewhere will talk about all of it, but what impressed me most of all was the "fierce, tenacious energy" it took to convey everything he wanted to convey. Kenji Ozawa is being born again, right now, all over again. It made me happy to feel like I was sharing in that moment.

But setting all of that aside for now. In the set that had been handed out to everyone in the audience beforehand, there was a postcard and a pencil. Write something and send it, he said. He'd asked so earnestly that I made up my mind to do it. And so the story circles back to where it began.

Reading a postcard might bring joy, but writing one feels good too. Scribbling down in a hurry how wonderful it all was, then half-jogging to the mailbox in the middle of the night, that was a thrill of its own. I imagine hundreds of postcards from people just like me will pile up. Picturing Kenji Ozawa sitting there reading them all, I thought: what an enviable way to end a concert.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, too. Handing out postage-paid postcards to the audience... that's something I'd love to steal someday.
entry.summary: A concert attendee describes the joy of being moved to immediately mail a handwritten postcard to the artist, capturing how this simple, personal gesture created a meaningful connection between performer and audience that transcended the typical concert experience.

entry.title: On being a copywriter.
entry.content:
At a certain point, I quit my job as an advertising copywriter. It was always the kind of job where you only had to call yourself one, so saying I "quit" is a bit odd. What I really stopped doing was accepting assignments from clients. Back then, whenever someone labeled me "copywriter" in a bio or introduction, I'd say, if I thought of it, "I'm not really doing that anymore, so please just put 'Hobonichi representative.'" Eventually, correcting people every time became a hassle, and I figured if that's how they wanted to see me, I might as well let them. And before long, I came to think of what I'm doing now as more or less the work of a copywriter anyway, just on commission from ourselves.

The word "copywriter" used to be translated into Japanese as something like "advertising text-and-concept maker." The job, in other words, is to create "text and concept" for advertising. But of those two elements, "text" is the more visible one, and "concept" is harder to see in tangible form, so people tend to think of it as "someone who writes clever sentences." Back in the Edo or Meiji periods, advertising copy probably existed as one branch of popular literary composition. But times gradually changed, and while the output may still take the form of "text," the "concept" side grew far more important.

As "concept" expands and deepens further, what's really being called for is the concept in the fuller sense. The standard translation is something like "an abstract idea," but it's more like: "If you look at it this way, here's what you see." Concepts can hit or miss, of course. Say you're trying to find a concept for sumo wrestling: you might land on "an athletic organization expressed through the aesthetics of period drama." Not the most practical concept, I'll admit.

So if a copywriter's job is to think up concepts, then I've been doing copywriting for Hobonichi all along, on my own, without interruption. (Will this continue? Maybe.)

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. As for the "text" side of things, as opposed to the "concept" side, I think that requires its own kind of training entirely.
entry.summary: The author argues that copywriting has evolved from primarily crafting clever text to developing deeper conceptual frameworks—a shift that allowed her to seamlessly transition from advertising work to her current role creating concepts for Hobonichi without truly changing professions.

entry.title: Things done, things not done, and things left half-done.
entry.content:
When someone has seen something through to the end, or kept at something for a long time, we tend to say that's really something. I have things like that myself. This daily column for Hobonichi, for instance, I've kept going without a break for close to twenty-eight years now.

And then there must be plenty of things I've never done at all. Well, of course there are. Most things, I haven't done. To use my own example: I've never taken up golf. Once, just once, a friend invited me out to the course. Afterward, I never had the slightest feeling I'd do it again. I was invited on two separate occasions, and even received a set of clubs as a gift. But in the end, I never played. Mahjong was the same. I'd be sitting in the room while friends played, with no desire whatsoever to join in, just reading a book. I've played a handful of times over the course of my life, but I have no fond memories of it, and no urge to play again.

Golf and mahjong are just the obvious examples. The things I don't do are essentially infinite. Among them are things I've deliberately decided not to do.

But beyond doing and not doing, there's a third category. Things I started but couldn't keep up. In my case, there are actually a great many of these.

Let me dredge up some you probably haven't heard about. Take the shamisen, for instance. I got it into my head that I wanted to play the musical introductions for rakugo performances myself. So I bought one in Kyoto, found a teacher, and got ready to begin lessons. After just one session, I was done. Hopeless. Impossible. On days the teacher was supposed to come, I'd slip out the back door. Instruments in general have been a total loss, and anything involving exercise lasted two sessions at most. Language study, lessons of any kind, the moment I commit, I'm already looking for the exit. Just the other day, I even gave up sunbathing, using rain and strong wind as my excuse.

"Then why do you keep trying?" you might ask. Well, every now and then, something like fishing, or Hobonichi, sneaks in there, something I end up loving for a long time, something that actually sticks.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, too. They say someone who quits after three days is a quitter, but you could also argue: at least they lasted three days.
entry.summary: The essay explores the author's lifelong pattern of starting and abandoning pursuits, contrasting the rare activities that do stick—like his twenty-eight-year commitment to writing a daily column—with the countless failed attempts at hobbies like golf, mahjong, and musical instruments. Through this reflection, he suggests that perseverance is less about willpower and more about discovering those rare things that naturally resonate with us.

entry.title: Life is a mass of ongoing thought.
entry.content:
After all my work and errands are done, I open my Hobonichi Techo and write down whatever was on my mind that day. But the thing is, by the time everything is truly finished, it's already the very last sliver of time before I really ought to be asleep. So I finally realized: that's why I'm always so drowsy when I write. I use a fountain pen in my Hobonichi Techo, but what ends up on the page is always the worst handwriting I'm capable of producing. I've been thinking what a shame that is. Maybe I should write in the Techo earlier, and then do my work or take my bath afterward. But no, if I did that, the time would feel too open-ended, and I'd be stuck wondering what to write. This is a matter I'll need to think about further.

On a certain cycle, the books in our house reach a point of overflow. I'm aware that my superior has been deploying tactics like what I'd call "simplified spiriting-away" to quietly deal with the situation somehow. But when even that isn't enough, she issues an order along the lines of "do something about these," rephrased in slightly softer terms. She'll say something to the effect of, "I could go ahead and pull together the candidates for removal myself, if you'd like," delivered in her feminine voice. This is deeply distressing. It's not something that can be decided so easily. Books I might read, books I might never read, sitting there like stones on a riverbank, forming a landscape. That is the world that "books and I" have built together. There isn't a single book I can point to and say, "I'll definitely never read this." Which is exactly why, for people like me, events like the Maebashi BOOK FES are so necessary. If the person who receives a book is happy to have it, then I'm willing to let it go. This, too, is a matter I'll need to think about further.

I have a lot of berets, so I decided to start wearing them. I'd been putting one on almost every day, and my hair started developing a permanent crease from it. My hairdresser K pointed this out to me. She suggested that perhaps wearing a hat only occasionally would be better. It occurred to me that she'd done a skillful job cutting my hair, and it must be a little disappointing for her to see it hidden under a hat all the time.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. There's no shortage of tiny, seemingly trivial dilemmas still under consideration.
entry.summary: The essay explores the author's small, everyday struggles—from finding the right time to journal despite exhaustion, to deciding which books to keep, to balancing personal habits with consideration for others—revealing that life's most persistent dilemmas are often the seemingly trivial ones we continuously revisit.

entry.title: Do you like a 50% win rate?
entry.content:
A fifty-percent win rate means no surplus and no deficit. No matter how many games you play, the wins and losses come out even. I never had a very good image of that. Especially in sports, you can't win a championship with a fifty-percent record. So whenever my favorite team was sitting at .500, I'd feel a little dejected, thinking, "They just can't pull it together."

But lately, maybe because I've grown up a bit, I've started to think, "You know, fifty percent isn't so bad."

If your wins and losses are equal, does that mean, to put it bluntly, "you might as well not have bothered"? But then I realized: that's also exactly the same as standing at the starting line, thinking, "Here we go." Having no surplus and no debt, about to begin something new. That situation is, in essence, identical to a fifty-percent win rate.

And while I'm at it, if you've played a lot of games and come out with no surplus and no deficit, it also means you've accumulated experience. It means you've been alive and moving through all that time.

To be honest, as someone who simply loves sports, I'm not satisfied with fifty percent. I can say without hesitation that I love a runaway championship victory. That's true. But I want to nurture something like a respect for that fifty-percent record.

"A .500 team you desperately want to root for." "A .500 team that feels like it could do more." "A .500 team where you can really sense the teamwork." If we could feel those things, and make others feel them too, I think there'd be so much more to enjoy.

Well, sports are a kind of stand-in for battle, so winning and losing will always matter. But in everything else we do, I think it would be wonderful if we could pull off a .500 record that has real charm.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. Ambition and acceptance. Fight and peace. Passion and a gentle smile. I want them all.
entry.summary: While a .500 record may seem like failure, it actually represents a fresh start and the accumulated experience of having lived fully, deserving respect and appreciation rather than disappointment. The essay argues for finding meaning and charm in balanced, middling results—both in sports and life—rather than constantly chasing victory.

entry.title: I have something I'm quietly working hard at.
entry.content:
I had planned to just keep at it quietly, but I can't hold it in any longer, so here it is. I bought a brand-new pair of jeans, and I've been wearing them into the ground, trying to break them in until they're fully seasoned. Not pre-distressed. Not someone else's well-worn pair from a vintage shop. I want to wear them myself until they look just right.

Back around the time Hobonichi first launched, I was doing exactly this, in earnest. They grew into something that looked really good, but because I spend so much time sitting at a desk, the seat wore through and got far too thin. I've forgotten a lot of the details from back then, but I think I must have worn them hard for close to a year. The truly devoted types apparently never even wash theirs, but I'm a member of society, more or less, so I washed mine as I went. Even those memories are now a quarter century old.

I own a great many pairs of jeans, so I'd never committed to picking one pair and wearing them day after day. Now that I actually want to start from scratch, it turns out to be surprisingly difficult. It takes real, conscious effort. It's not that I have some particular desire to look stylish, but I'm also not trying to play the stubborn old crank who wears the same jeans every day. I simply felt like doing it. It's probably not so different from thinking, maybe I'll take up fishing, or maybe I'll start running.

This may sound a bit abstract, but the accumulation of each day's hours gets recorded in a single pair of jeans, as creases, as scuffs, as fading, becoming a kind of expression. No one else can buy the same thing, and no one else can make it. And yet here I am, an old man by now, wanting to go out of my way to try this all over again.

I'm about three months in. There are changes, though no one else would notice. It's right around the stage where you might or might not detect the first signs of them starting to look right. Am I bored with it? Of course, I got bored ages ago. But having written about it today, I feel like I can keep going.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, as always. I have a feeling I might actually be suited to simple repetition.
entry.summary: The author describes breaking in a new pair of jeans through daily wear as a meditative practice that creates something uniquely personal—a record of time and effort that cannot be replicated or purchased. Though the process is mundane and requires conscious commitment, he finds meaning in this simple act of repetition and the subtle transformations it produces.

entry.title: Hoping for hope.
entry.content:
Most people, I think, want hope. Though probably very few would put it into those exact words, openly wishing for it: "I want hope." To be honest, I myself had never said such a thing. I simply noticed that somewhere deep down, the feeling was there.

Every day, a relentless stream of news reaches us. So much of it is bad. And most of it is probably true, so you take it in as best you can. What to do about any of it is never easy to figure out. Ideas and projections pour in too, about the state of things now, about what might be coming next. You absorb them, but it's hard to imagine much of it leading anywhere good.

Wait, I thought. I can't see any hope at all in any of this. That was the moment it hit me. Among everything being reported, everything I was learning, everything I was being made to think about, there was so remarkably little that pointed toward hope.

Well then, was there more hope in the past? Hmm, I suppose hope has always been hard to find. But when I saw, say, a middle school kid throwing everything they had into something, or anyone who seemed to carry hope inside them, there it was. Hope was right there.

Maybe that's the thing. Maybe people who have hope are themselves the hope.

You can try all you want to make someone understand just how bleak the world is. A person who carries hope will go right on carrying it. Sure, there are con artists who wave hope around like a banner. And people who crush hope, people who mock it. Of course there are. But even knowing all that, hope doesn't just disappear. There are no grounds for hope, no guarantees. A baby's very first cry is hope itself.

I've decided, all over again, to be someone who looks for hope.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. A child running full speed through the park. A person whose face breaks into a smile when they say good morning.
entry.summary: In a world saturated with bleak news and grim projections, hope isn't found in circumstances but in the people who embody it—those who choose to carry it forward despite having no rational guarantee. The author decides to become someone who actively seeks out and recognizes hope in small human moments, understanding that hopeful people are themselves the hope the world needs.

entry.title: Is everyone doing well? Or not so much?
entry.content:
The term "May sickness" has been around for a long, long time. April brings the new school year, new jobs, the end of winter with temperatures climbing, all sorts of changes in your environment, and then, almost without warning (though it's not really a surprise), Golden Week arrives. When that string of holidays ends, you're thrown back into a daily life you haven't quite gotten used to yet. Kid or adult, it doesn't matter. Of course you're going to feel off your game. They say that all change, every kind, is stress. A new environment, difficult circumstances, sure, but apparently even things that make you happier than before count as stress. By that logic, even the beautiful clear skies of May are stressful.

When you put it all together, I have to say with a wry laugh: is there really anyone who's full of energy in May? What about you? Oh, me? I'm a mess, of course. I wouldn't call it "May sickness," but still. When I ask the people around me, just griping together, they all say, as if citing some universal law, "May is generally no good." I knew it, I think, and somehow we bond over our shared misery.

I can't speak for the Meiji era or the Edo period, but my feeling is that May in Japan has always been a time when people fall out of sorts. If that's the case, then surely, as a kind of survival wisdom, there must be some tradition or observance designed to help us get through this rough patch. Oh, wait. There is. The Boys' Festival, Tango no Sekku. Because illness tends to spread at the turn of the seasons, people would display warrior dolls to ward off evil spirits and hang carp streamers to swim in the wind, pouring their hopes into them.

Right. I'd been so fixated on the holidays themselves that I forgot to pay proper attention to the festival as a seasonal rite. Well, the best I can come up with at this point is that maybe I should eat more kashiwa-mochi.

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. This grumbly tone of mine might be the truest expression of my May.
entry.summary: May brings accumulated stress from seasonal changes and the end of holidays, leaving most people exhausted and out of sorts—a phenomenon so universal in Japan that traditional festivals like Tango no Sekku were created as survival wisdom to help people endure this difficult month.

entry.title: The curious expressive form known as the memo.
entry.content:
Someday, once I've thought it through a little more, I'll write about it. That's the kind of thing I jot down as a memo. And sometimes I really do think it through and write it up properly. But other times, even after thinking a little more, I find I can't write it after all, and the memo just sits there.

I'm not great at thinking things through rigorously, and sure, there's something like an inferiority complex in that. But lately I've started to wonder if maybe that's not really the issue.

Back when I was working as a copywriter, I wrote a line like "Delicious Life." Beyond that, I wrote a few lines of body copy, but it was hardly an essay, just a handful of words. If someone had told me at the time, "Explain it in more detail," would I have launched into some long-winded commentary on the copy? I was interviewed about that line a number of times, but I was never, ever able to say anything that truly satisfied the interviewer. And now I think that was just fine.

Think of haiku, or tanka, or short verse. Someone once wrote the poem, "The old pond, a frog jumps in, the sound of water." That poem doesn't need body copy. And there's certainly no need to sit down and develop it into a thesis. Still, if the author wanted to talk about those seventeen syllables, they could go on as long as they liked. I think that's how it works.

I'm fully aware this is self-serving, but even though a short memo isn't a recognized art form, I came to think, "Maybe it's fine just as it is." If you want to explain further what's written there, go ahead. And if you don't, just put it out into the world anyway.

Freedom, and how extraordinary a thing it is, can be understood from a single fact: the deprivation of freedom is what we use as punishment.

...That was a memo, set down just as it was. If I tried to continue, I think it would only get tedious.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. And come to think of it, I probably could have said even that more briefly, as a memo.
entry.summary: The author argues that brief, incomplete observations and memos need not be fully developed into essays to have value—sometimes a fragmentary insight, like haiku or a single sentence, can stand perfectly well on its own without elaboration or justification.

entry.title: When moods feel alike.
entry.content:
Yesterday, I got to see Moegara at the "Kayō Kissa" event, so I went ahead and told him something I'd been dying to say. That the main character of the Korean drama I'm watching most intently right now, "Everyone Is Fighting Their Own Worthlessness," reminds me of him. It's not exactly that their faces look alike. If you put their photos side by side, they might not resemble each other at all. Actually, when I just now searched for a photo of "Ku Gyohwan," who plays the lead, and compared it with one of Moegara, they're not entirely unlike each other. And come to think of it, Masayoshi Yamazaki looks somewhat similar too. The face and hairstyle may be part of it, of course, but more than anything, it's the mood that's similar. Something about unspoken preferences, or how they want to exist in this world.

Moegara hasn't seen the drama, so naturally he neither agreed nor disagreed, but I really do want him to watch it. The only trouble is, in the first episode, the main character, Hwang Dong-man, appears as an incredibly obnoxious guy, so it might just come across as "Moegara at his absolute worst." I can't really blame him if he takes it that way. Even I started watching with some distance, thinking, "If someone like this were actually in my life, it'd be a real problem."

Anyway, I think it's the writing that makes the show so good. Let me pick out a scene the main character isn't even in. For example, there's a moment where a married couple, both minor characters, are having an argument. The husband, a film director, gets angry: "Do you even love me?" His wife, who also happens to be the head of the production company and therefore his boss, fires back: "Twenty years of marriage, and you're asking about love? Saying 'no' wouldn't hurt me. Saying 'yes' wouldn't do me any good. Think it over carefully." The husband is left completely off balance.

I've taken the thread of our conversation at "Kayō Kissa" and continued it right here. Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. And just like that, Golden Week draws to a close.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on watching a Korean drama that reminds the author of a friend, ultimately praising the show's sophisticated writing that explores complex human relationships and emotional authenticity beyond surface-level characterization.

entry.title: Are you finding something you can lose yourself in?
entry.content:
Being completely absorbed in something... it really is a wonderful thing. If you're caught up in something right now, that time is a treasure. Please, hold on to it. Though honestly, what might be even more amazing is when you're so absorbed that the question of whether you're absorbed or not doesn't even cross your mind.

When children are lost in play, it's like they become one with whatever they're doing. You tell them, "All right, that's enough now," and they don't hear a word of it.

Music, manga, games, sports. Honestly, I think it doesn't matter what it is.

I started reading a book once and it got so interesting that I secretly kept reading until the middle of the night. I have never, ever forgotten that. It was Kita Morio's *Doctor Manbo's Book of Insects*.

An elementary school kid staying up until the small hours is obviously not great for their health, and you wouldn't want it to become a habit. Books aren't going anywhere. You can slip in a bookmark and pick it up again the next day. And a responsible adult is right to tell a child exactly that.

Still, the feeling I had that night is something I count among my greatest possessions. The short hand of the clock had moved to a place it never usually reached while I was awake. I was lying in bed, unable to stop reading. I don't remember much beyond that. I probably didn't finish the book that night. "The middle of the night" was most likely one in the morning at the latest. Even so, that experience of being completely absorbed was enormously important to me.

As an adult, sure, I've lost track of time with games, TV dramas, and sometimes even work. And right now, more than ever, I feel a strong urge to affirm all of it, every last instance.

My own capacity for that kind of total absorption has grown softer somewhere along the way.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi today. Better than learning is enjoying. Better than enjoying is playing. That goes for grown-ups too.
entry.summary: Complete absorption in an activity—whether as a child or adult—is a precious and increasingly rare gift that shouldn't be dismissed or lost as we grow older. The essay celebrates those moments when we're so fully engaged that time disappears, arguing they're among life's greatest treasures worthy of protection and affirmation.

entry.title:
An idle mind is the devil's workshop — just your typical long weekend.
Hmm, let me reconsider to keep it more natural and closer to the original:
It's a long weekend, so I'm just idling about getting into mischief.
entry.content:
During a long holiday, there's no time spent moving from one thing to the next, following a schedule, asking yourself "What's next again?" And that's a very good thing. Spending time in that unnameable, drifting way is actually what matters most. But at the same time, it also means you're prone to focusing on things you'd rather not think about.

Right now, having just said goodbye to a friend, my mind keeps turning in that direction. People say this often, but the death of someone close tends to show itself not so much as sadness but as loneliness. When you're buried in daily work, you don't have the luxury of sitting with that loneliness, and in a way, that's a mercy. But, though I can't blame the holidays exactly, having all this free time means I end up not feeling so great.

In that sense, the "Kayō Kissa" event at Shibuya Parco is a godsend. You go there, and the old pop songs fill the air, and all you have to do is let yourself be carried along by the music. Even if you look like you're just staring into space, you're not really idle. On May 4th it's my turn to do a talk, so I won't get to sit back and enjoy it from the audience, but I plan to enjoy it anyway. I'll be playing songs that are old even for someone my age, so the audience might want to brace themselves.

The truth is, if Eiichi Ohtaki were still around, he would have been the perfect person to talk with about all this. But he's another one who's gone to the other side.

This might seem abrupt, but I've been wondering: where and how is the word "believe" being used these days? Is it treated like some baseless emotion, something people dismiss with, "That's just what you choose to believe"? After all, it has nothing to do with so-called evidence or data. Our modern era has done a pretty thorough job of pushing religion, that part of human culture rooted in belief, into a far corner. I'd like to take another look at this old-fashioned word, "believe." Though it's almost too big a subject to know where to begin.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. The whole country is on holiday with idle hands and idle minds, so it might be a tricky time for everyone.
entry.summary: During a long holiday, free time can intensify loneliness and difficult emotions, yet the author explores how shared experiences like music and meaningful conversation—grounded in belief and human connection—offer solace when we're most vulnerable to our thoughts.

entry.title:
I'm sorry, but I don't see any specific Japanese text to translate. Could you please provide the Japanese essay expression you'd like me to translate into natural English?
If you're looking for a general example, please share the specific text and I'll be happy to help translate it.
entry.content:
There's an event called "Kayōkyoku Kissaten" running at Shibuya Parco's "Hobonichi Sunday," and yesterday's guest was Mino from "Mino Music," so I'd reserved a seat in the audience. The thing is, though, there was another event I'd been looking forward to for ages on this very same day, May 2nd: the Inoue Naoya versus Nakatani Junpei world title match. I realized they'd fall on the same day at the same time, but I decided to prioritize our own "scene" and head to Shibuya. And of course, it was a wonderful time. But even rushing home afterward, I wouldn't get back until around nine. I figured the fight would already be over, but... wait, it hadn't even started yet. Are you serious? I actually made it in time?! So thanks to that, I got the full experience, from Mino straight through to Inoue-Nakatani, no breaks, all titles dropped.

The fight was incredible. "Breathtaking" doesn't begin to cover it. Normally when I'm watching a boxing match on TV, I keep glancing at the countdown clock showing the three-minute rounds. But with Inoue and Nakatani going at each other, I couldn't look away for a single moment. I may never have had a viewing experience like that before. What someone like me, a total amateur, means when I say "that was incredible" is probably on a completely different level from what people who truly understand boxing were feeling. But when both perspectives arrive at "that was incredible," that's the mark of an ideal fight. I have a feeling this one will go down as legendary.

There's something impressive about things that are "amazing to those who know." But sometimes that overlaps with things that are "amazing even to those who don't." There's a kind of greatness only experts can appreciate, and there's a kind of goodness that draws in ordinary people. And things that genuinely have both? They really do exist. Years ago, at a certain company, the elevator doors opened and I caught sight of a monochrome sketch hanging on the wall of the lobby. Something about it struck me as remarkably good, and I actually got back in the elevator just to go up and see it again. When I found out it was a Picasso, I thought, well, of course. The same goes for Ryōkan's calligraphy, even printed in a magazine, or the poetry of Mado Michio. That kind of thing.

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. Every time I encounter something truly good, it makes me want to enjoy my own work all the more.
entry.summary: The author reflects on how genuine greatness—whether in a boxing match, art, or poetry—transcends expertise to move both specialists and ordinary people alike, inspiring deeper appreciation for quality work across all domains.

entry.title: But honestly, I don't really care either way.
entry.content:
Realistically, we're eating at night, so I suppose there's no helping it if people call dinner "night-meal" instead of "evening-meal." Families with small children might serve dinner on the early side to get them to bed sooner, but even then, if it's six-thirty or seven o'clock, you could already call that night. Still, I plan to keep saying "evening-meal" for at least a few more years. Oh, and what about the question you get at a traditional inn: "What time would you like your evening meal?" That's probably not going to change. After all, there's also the late-night snack, so you can't go using up the word "night" too early.

On an entirely different note, very few people actually pronounce the Japanese word for "queen" correctly. The word is a compound of "woman" and "king," but somewhere along the way an extra syllable crept in, and almost everyone mispronounces it with the sounds in the wrong order. To their credit, NHK announcers are careful to say it properly. Maybe they have special training for that sort of thing.

Speaking of which, and this is a continuation of the same kind of thing, most people seem to slur the pronunciation of "NHK" itself. When there's an earthquake and someone wants to check the emergency news, they'll say something like "Put on NHK," but with a lazy, compressed version of the letters. Or maybe that's just how it sounds to me. It does seem like very few people enunciate each letter crisply. At this point, I think that's just how it goes, and that's fine.

That said, there are cases where it's probably not fine. For instance, when people mean "to go on and on endlessly," they'll sometimes use the word for "eternity" instead of the correct word, which means something like "at great length." The two are close enough in feeling, both suggesting something that stretches on and on, so I can understand the mix-up. Maybe in two hundred years or so, using "eternity" that way will be officially accepted.

Anyway, I just felt like writing down some things I don't usually talk about. Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. And for the record, my own Japanese is far from proper.
entry.summary: The essay reflects on various minor linguistic quirks and mispronunciations in Japanese—from calling dinner a "night-meal" to misusing "eternity" for "at great length"—while acknowledging that language naturally evolves and that rigid adherence to correctness isn't always necessary. The author accepts most of these shifts as inevitable language change, even while personally preferring certain traditional usages.
